coredata.dc:title: Carbon dots supported single Fe atom nanozyme for drug-resistant glioblastoma therapy by activating autophagy-lysosome pathway
coredata.dc:creator: Muhammad, Pir
Hanif, Sumaira
Li, Jingyun
Guller, Anna
Rehman, Fawad Ur
Ismail, Muhammad
Zhang, Dongya
Yan, Xiyun
Fan, Kelong
Shi, Bingyang
coredata.dc:description: Glioblastoma (GBM) is a fatal and recurrent brain cancer without any complete prevailing remedy. Here, we explored single-atom nanozyme-mediated catalytic therapy to precisely target drug-resistant GBM via the lysosomal-mediated autophagic cell death pathway. The ultrasmall carbon dots supported iron single-atom nanozyme (Fe-CDs) were rationally designed and developed, exhibiting six naturally occurring enzymes: oxidase, catalase, superoxide dismutase, and the peroxidase family (peroxidase, glutathione peroxidase, and thiol peroxidase). Importantly, Fe-CDs act as a drug-free nanomedicine that modulates the tumor microenvironment via reactive oxygen species regulation and lysosome-mediated autophagy owing to the multiple enzyme-mimic properties. In addition, we introduce BBB permeable and glioma targeting peptides on Fe-CDs via surface modification for selectively GBM targeting in vivo. Our findings suggest that the cascade enzymatic activities of Fe-CDs stimulate autophagy to effectively inhibit tumor growth in drug-resistant GBM mice models. Thus, the new-generation Fe-CDs present great potential to be a robust and versatile remedial nanoplatform with minimal toxicity and high potency for precise drug-resistant GBM therapy.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Characterization of Fe-CDs nanozyme. (a) TEM image of the Fe-CDs nanozyme, the inset is the calculated average diameter (nm) of particles. (b) HRTEM image of the corresponding Fe-CDs nanozyme. (c) The elemental atomic contents are estimated from the TEM EDS mapping. (d) Full survey XPS spectra of Fe-CDs nanozyme. XPS extended spectra of C1s (e), Fe2p scan (f), and N1s scan (g) of the fabricated Fe-CDs. (h) Excitation-emission 3D matrices of the Fe-CDs nanozyme.

Atomic structural characterization of Fe-CDs nanozyme. XANES spectra of Fe (a) and N (b) K-edge in Fe-CDs nanozyme. (c) Fourier transform of Fe K-edge EXAFS spectra of Fe-CDs-nanozyme, FeO, Fe-N (heme), Fe2O3, and Fe foil. (d) Wavelet transforms (WT) contour plots of the k3-weighted EXAFS data of Fe-CDs and reference samples (FePc (Fe-N in heme) and Fe foil). (e) k2-weighted Fourier transforms EXAFS spectra of Fe-CDs, Fe foil, Fe-N (heme, FePc). (f) The corresponding EXAFS fitting curves of Fe-CDs-nanozyme at r space. Inset: a model for the surface of Fe-CDs nanozyme. Iron: Fe (red), Nitrogen: N (blue), and Carbon: C (gray). (g) Fitting curves of the EXAFS of Fe-CDs-nanozyme in the K space.

Enzyme kinetics of N-CDs and Fe-CDs nanozymes. Kinetics for OXD-like (a), POD-like (b), SOD-like (c), CAT-like (d), and GPx-like (e) activities of N-CDs and Fe-CDs nanozymes. (f) Schematic illustration of enzyme-mimicking activities of Fe-CDs nanozyme (upper). The H2O2 removal mechanism of our Fe-CDs nanozyme with enhanced GPx-like properties (below) under the GR (glutathione reductase) coupled reaction. Generation of •OH (g) and O2 • (h) radicals during catalysis. ESR analysis was carried out under the following conditions: 100 mM H2O2, 20 mM DMPO, and 0.05 mg/mL of Fe-CDs/N-CDs nanozymes, in 100 mM acetate buffer (pH= 4.5). Error bars represent the standard error derived from three independent measurements.

The nanozymes-mediated killing pathway in GBM cells (U87MG and U251-TR) and the biodistribution of Fe-CDs@Ang in orthotopic GBM mice model. (a) Scheme of intracellular working mechanism for nanozyme mediated autophagosome and mitophagy cascade to induce cell death. (b) TEM image of Fe-CDs@Ang (60 μg/mL) treated GBM cells. Fe-CDs@Ang contained in the lysosome (red arrow) and damaged/swelled mitochondria (green arrows). The bottom images show enlarged fused autophagosome-lysosome (scale bar: 200 nm). (c) Schematic model and TEM image of double membraned autophagosome (autolysosome formation) in U87MG cell (scale bar: 200 nm). Red arrow: fussed vesicles to form autophagosome. (d) Flow cytometry analyses of annexin V-FITC/PI double-stained U251T-TR cells before and after treated with N-CDs, Fe-CDs, and Fe-CDs@Ang (200 µg/mL). (e) ROS-mediated apoptosis/necrosis protein markers expression including Bcl-2, HIF-a, and CypA by immunoblotting against the β-actin as control. (f) Representative western blots of autophagy-associated proteins (LC3-I, LC3-II, p62/SQSTM1, and Beclin 1) expression in GBM cells. Samples: i) Control, ii) N-CDs, iii) Fe-CDs, iv) Fe-CDs@Ang and v) Fe-CDs@Ang pre-treated NAC. (g) In vitro Transwell model of BBB and calculated transport ratio for applied groups (n = 3). (h) Fluorescence images of orthotopic U87MG-Luc tumor-bearing nude mice at different time points following injection of Fe-CDs and Fe-CDs@Ang (dosage: 10 mg/kg). (i) CDs tissue distribution post 6 h i.v. injection of N-CDs, Fe-CDs, and Fe-CDs@Ang in BALB/c nude mice (dosage:10 mg/kg). (j) Quantification of CDs (% ID/g) accumulated in different organs after N-CDs, Fe-CDs, and Fe-CDs@Ang post-injection for 6 h. Statistical P values were calculated by the two-tailed Student’s t-test. ***p < 0.001 (n = 4).

In vivo anti-tumor efficacy of Fe-CDs@Ang nanozymes. (a) In vivo therapeutic protocol of Fe-CDs@Ang nanozymes on GBM tumor-bearing nude mice. (b) Bioluminescence images of orthotopic U87MG-Luc tumor-bearing nude mice following treatment with PBS, Ang-Mal-PEG2k-NHS), N-CDs, Fe-CDs, and Fe-CDs@Ang by using Lumina IVIS III system (Dosage:10 mg/kg). (c) Quantified luminescence levels of mice using the Lumina IVIS III system. Body weight changes in orthotropic U87MG (d) and U251-TR (e) mice models. Mice survival rates using orthotropic models of U87MG (f) and U251-TR (g) tumor-bearing mice. Data are presented as mean SD (n = 6, one-way ANOVA and Tukey multiple comparisons tests, * p < 0.05, *** p < 0.001). (h) Tumor slices were excised from orthotopic U251-TR tumor-bearing nude mice following treatment using H&E, TUNEL, and CC3 apoptosis (scale bar: 50 µm). (i) Tumor slices excised from orthotopic U251-TR treated IHC for autophagy-mediated markers (p62 and LC3) staining (scale bar: 50 µm).

Schematic illustration of the preparation of Fe-CDs nanozyme and the enzymatic cascade initiated by angiopep-2 modified Fe-CDs nanozyme for ROS regulation to induce the autophagy-lysosome pathway for GBM therapy.

head.ce:title: Carbon dots supported single Fe atom nanozyme for drug-resistant glioblastoma therapy by activating autophagy-lysosome pathway

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Glioblastoma (GBM) is a fatal and recurrent brain cancer without any complete prevailing remedy. Here, we explored single-atom nanozyme-mediated catalytic therapy to precisely target drug-resistant GBM via the lysosomal-mediated autophagic cell death pathway. The ultrasmall carbon dots supported iron single-atom nanozyme (Fe-CDs) were rationally designed and developed, exhibiting six naturally occurring enzymes: oxidase, catalase, superoxide dismutase, and the peroxidase family (peroxidase, glutathione peroxidase, and thiol peroxidase). Importantly, Fe-CDs act as a drug-free nanomedicine that modulates the tumor microenvironment via reactive oxygen species regulation and lysosome-mediated autophagy owing to the multiple enzyme-mimic properties. In addition, we introduce BBB permeable and glioma targeting peptides on Fe-CDs via surface modification for selectively GBM targeting in vivo. Our findings suggest that the cascade enzymatic activities of Fe-CDs stimulate autophagy to effectively inhibit tumor growth in drug-resistant GBM mice models. Thus, the new-generation Fe-CDs present great potential to be a robust and versatile remedial nanoplatform with minimal toxicity and high potency for precise drug-resistant GBM therapy.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: All data needed to evaluate the conclusions in the paper are present in the paper and/or the Supplementary Materials. Additional data related to this paper may be requested from the authors.

ce:section.ce:para: Glioblastoma (GBM) is considered the most lethal and prevalent malignant tumor in the brain, comprising half of the diagnosed central nervous system (CNS) tumors [1,2]. GBM is described by poor clinical prognosis, with a high incidence of recurrences and high mortality rates [3,4]. Despite the recent grand efforts, the median survival time of the patients with GBM is less than 15 months after the diagnosis, and the five-year survival rate could hardly be reached up to 5% [5,6]. The discovery of novel and targeted therapeutic strategies for the successful treatment of GBM remained a pressing challenge in cancer therapy. The current standard of care for GBM patients includes surgical resection of the tumor combined with radiotherapy and adjuvant chemotherapy [7]. However, the complete surgical removal of GBM is almost impossible due to the highly invasive nature of GBM [8]. In addition, GBM is highly aggressive in nature and rapidly invades into the delicate anatomical structure of the brain, which leads to irreversible damage and infeasible cure [9]. The pharmacological GBM treatment strategies, in turn, are challenged because the brain parenchyma is shielded by a specialized histoanatomical structure known as the blood-brain barrier (BBB). BBB is mainly responsible for the inability of traditional chemotherapeutics to reach brain tumors [10,11]. Next, the highly adaptive nature of GBM cells results in rapidly progressing drug- and radioresistance of cancer, another factor contributing to treatment failure [12,13].

The unique features of nanozymes offer a plausible way to bridge the gap between nanozymes and their natural analogs. For example, Ji et al. has demonstrated the metal-organic framework (MOF)-based SAzyme with metal coordination within heterocyclic nitrogen that was used to impart the catalytic features [34]. However, the nanozyme-based catalytic therapies typically exhibit insufficient tumor selectivity, while the detailed mechanisms linking the ROS regulation by SAzyme and tumor death remain unclear. It is currently supported that nanozymes can control cellular fate by various effects on intracellular redox homeostasis [35]. These effects might result in the adaption of oxidative stress (activation/scavenge), leading to apoptosis and other complementary responses, e.g., autophagy [36]. Particularly, the active conversion of the substrate (H2O2) to radicals via nanozymes resulted in fluctuating the deprived oxygen level in the tumor cell to trigger the death of tumor cells [37]. Eventually, nanozymes also act as antioxidants to scavenge ROS that helps tailor the neuroinflammatory responses for the compensation of the brain edema [38]. However, the unique TME of GBM with hypoxic conditions and overexpressed antioxidant system featured highly adaptive cellular redox homeostasis [39]. In addition, the heterogeneous tumor structure represents a robust contemporary challenge for typical nanozymes to target GBM and chemo-resistive systems. Stimulation of autophagy, a natural self-eating lysosomal phenomenon in cells to eliminate redundant cellular components[40], has been successfully employed for cell death induction in numerous chemotherapies [41]. Importantly, it was detected that the chemo-resistive malignant GBM cells are more vulnerable to autophagic cell death pathway rather than apoptosis [42]. Hence, pro-autophagic stimulators can be an effective adjuvant to aid future therapies to fight against chemo-resistant malignant GBM [43]. To date, only a few studies have explored the nanozyme-mediated autophagic cell death in various cancer cell lines [44,45]. Recently, few studies demonstrated the use of heteroatom-doped carbon dots (CDs) to explore the autophagic cell death pathways [46,47]. However, enzyme mimics nanomaterials that initiate the autophagic cell death for tumor therapy are not well explored. Thus, we took advantage of nanozymes-based catalytic therapy to induce subsequent autophagic cell death pathway activation in drug-resistant GBM targeting.

Here, we presented an atomic-level engineered ultra-small Fe-doped carbon dots (Fe-CDs) nanozyme through controlling the single-atom iron (Fe) active center via precise coordination of the nitrogen (N) and carbon (C). The Fe-CDs nanozyme exhibit multiple enzymatic activities, including POD, OXD, SOD, CAT, TPx, and GPx, responsible for redox homeostasis ( Scheme 1). To further enable precise transport across the BBB, Fe-CDs were functionalized with angiopep-2 (now named Fe-CDs@Ang). Angiopep-2 is a class of synthetic low-density lipoprotein receptor-related protein 1 (LRP-1) ligand that is derived from the Kunitz domain and exhibits higher transcytosis capacity as a BBB shuttle peptide and GBM accumulation in brain parenchyma [48]. The Fe-CDs@Ang successfully target the LRP-1, over-expressed by brain capillary endothelial and tumor cells. Hence, substantial anti-tumor activity was achieved by an effective brain tumor accumulation of nanozyme via LRPs-mediated transcytosis/endocytosis across the BBB. The Fe-CDs@Ang nanozyme enabled specific ROS regulation in a TME, resulting in significant tumor regression in GBM xenograft mice models. It can precisely participate in ROS-mediated autophagy signaling pathways to overcome the drug resistance in solid GBM tumors. Fe-CDs@Ang accumulated within the acidic endosome-lysosome (pH 4–5) to show intrinsic OXD/POD-like activities that impair the lysosomal degradation capacity to activate the autophagic flux. In addition, the Fe-CDs@Ang also exert SOD, CAT, and GPx-like activities that act as a ROS regulator to enhance autophagy and lysosome-based apoptosis. It is well known that hypoxia is implicated as a co-regulator for autophagy activation to reprogram apoptotic tumor cell death [49]. Thus, activated autophagy pathways relieve hypoxia and disrupt the redox-hemostasis, further prompting the expression of enhanced apoptotic GBM cell death [39]. Notably, our study showed a rational design of atomic level nanozyme that regulates the autophagy to attenuate the GBM cell proliferation and offer an alternative strategy for an improved therapeutic effect in drug-resistant GBM. Finally, the improved treatment efficiency results in higher survival rates with reduced systemic adverse effects in orthotopic U87MG and drug-resistant U251-TR human glioblastoma tumor mice models.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and characterization of Fe–CDs/N-CDs nanozyme
ce:section.ce:para: The procedure for synthesizing Fe-CDs nanozyme via plant-derived carbon precursor is provided in Scheme S1. These biosynthesized Fe-CDs were prepared using a microwave-assisted pyrolysis procedure from the bougainvillea leaves extract. The plant extract was taken as a C and N source to prepare Fe-CDs without adding extra reactants. As per previously reported literature, the plant biomass is rich in carbohydrates, proteins, and other nitrogen-containing phytochemicals that have been successfully utilized to synthesize the plant-derived heteroatom doped CDs [50]. We used a similar green strategy because of its simplicity, biocompatibility, and scalability. In addition, this strategy does not require additional reactants for doping, surface passivation, or post-modification due to numerous molecules which provide sufficient C and N elements for the fabrication of Fe-CDs [51]. Thus, the addition of FeCl2 salt in the biosynthesis process of Fe-CDs resulted in Fe and N co-doping preferentially into the carbonized structure. The morphology and structure of Fe-CDs were confirmed by using transmission electron microscopy (TEM) analysis. The Fe-CDs were equally distributed in spherical size and well dispersed with an average diameter of 8.8 ± 4.916 nm ( Figs. 1a and S1a). To integrate the exact role of metal doping in the enzyme-mimicking characteristic for the anti-tumor effect, metal-free N-CDs nanozyme were also fabricated. The fabricated N-CDs shared the uniform morphologies with 7.5 ± 3.523 nm diameter with a polydispersity index (PDI) of 0.192 (Fig. S1b and c). The high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) image confirmed that Fe-CDs exhibit excellent crystallinity with an ordered fringe pattern (Fig. 1b). The lattice interval of Fe-CDs was determined as 0.220 nm, indicating the d-spacing of the interlayer separation of [100] facet of sp2 graphitic carbon pattern [52]. Moreover, other high index facets of (111), (200), and (311) were also recognized through selected area electron diffraction (SAED) patterns (Fig. S2a). The several rings in the SAED pattern specified that the region was composed of different arrangements of graphitic packing as interplanar fringes within the same sphere [53]. We further confirmed the chemical composition and crystalline nature of Fe-CDs by using atomic-resolution scanning transmission electron microscopy (STEM), accompanied by the high-angle annular dark-field (HAADF) mode. The HAADF-STEM analyses show that Fe-CDs exhibited the core particle-like structure, with the core in a bright shade (Fig. S2b). TEM-X-ray energy-dispersive spectrometer (EDS) mapping (Fig. S2c-S2g) verified the existence of uniformly distributed Fe, N, O, and C elements and their overlay scan in Fe-CDs. Next, the semi-quantitation of elements in Fe-CDs was analyzed by TEM-EDS and ICP-OES (inductively coupled plasma optical emission spectroscopy), as shown in Figs. 1c and S2h. The EDS spectra results show that the maximum Fe in Fe-CDs was quantified as 3.4% (% by mass), while the Fe doping ICP data plateau got flat at ~0.22 g dosage for the FeCl2 curve. These results indicated that doped Fe or N-CDs are well-dispersed in the core. Next, the high degree of crystallinity in Fe-CDs/N-CDs was confirmed using Raman spectrum analysis (Fig. S3a). The characteristic Raman peaks were localized in the 1000–2000 cm−1 region. The band at 1604 cm−1, denoted as G, indicated sp2 carbon networks, and the D-band at 1300 cm−1 reflected the defects or graphitized disorder structure. The intensity ratios of the D- to G-bands (ID/IG) for N-CDs and Fe-CDs were 1.43 and 1.52, respectively. We next determined the chemical composition of Fe-CDs/N-CDs using Fourier-transform infrared (FT-IR) and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) analysis. The FT-IR spectra of the Fe-CDs and N-CDs (Fig. S3b) suggested the existence of abundant oxygen and nitrogen-containing groups on their surfaces; mainly as hydroxyl (υ(O–H), υ(C-H) at 3200–3450 cm−1), carboxyl (υ(CO), C-C (sp2) peaks at 1670–1750 cm−1), carbonyl (υ(CO), and carbon-nitrogen (C-N) 1500–1630 cm−1). In comparison, the Fe-O peak has merely been detected in Fe-CDs at 525 cm−1. The complex surface functionalities existing on Fe-CDs were further investigated using XPS analysis. Fig. 1d showed a full survey scanned XPS spectra that verify the presence of the C1s (70.81%), O1s (13.4%), N1s (11.4%), Fe2p3/2 (2.7%), and Fe2p1/2 (1.69%), which corresponded to peaks at 284.0, 531.9, 400.01, 710 eV, respectively. In addition, the high-resolution XPS spectra over the deconvolution demonstrated the presence of different Fe-binding sites within C and N atoms. Fig. 1e presented the deconvolution displays for high-resolution XPS short scan of C1s that indicated the multiple C-binding sites. Fig. 1f exhibited the first doublet at 710.2, and 723.5 eV corresponds to the binding energies of Fe2+ 2p3/2 and Fe2+ 2p1/2, indicating the presence of Fe2+ in Fe-CDs structure. The binding energy at around 714.3 and 728.3 eV belonged to satellite peaks of Fe-Nx, and Fe-O peaks in the Fe 2p XPS spectrum. Moreover, the N1s spectrum for Fe-CDs comprised various peaks at 397.86, 398.77, 400.23, and 402.5 eV, which could be attributed to pyridinic, Fe-N, pyrrolic, graphitic, and oxidized nitrogen, as shown in Fig. 1g. As literature confirmed that the pyrrolic and pyridinic nitrogen atoms had the probability of coordinating with Fe to produce Fe-Nx active site [54]. These data verified the successful doping of Fe via nitrogen coordination in Fe-CDs-nanozyme. The atomic structure and doping as active sites play a vital role in catalytic reaction performance [55].

The optical properties of Fe-CDs/N-CDs were investigated by UV–vis (ultraviolet-visible) and fluorescence spectroscopy. The UV–vis absorption spectrum of Fe-CDs exhibited a robust characteristic absorption band centered at about 665 nm (Fig. S4a). Three typical absorption peaks of Fe-CDs at 313, 413, and 436 nm correspond to the π–π * and n–π * transitions state of the sp2 aromatic graphitic framework for CC, CO, and CN bonds [56]. The absorption peak at ∼670 nm originated due to charge transfer between the metal–ligand (Fe-N/Fe-O) bond [57]. Similarly, the fluorescence spectroscopy of Fe-CDs provides an excitation-dependent emission in the range of 400–580 nm, which gives a shift of 10 nm in the fluctuated emission wavelength (Fig. 1h). The slight change in emission wavelength conferred only one kind of luminescent center in a near-infrared (NIR) fluorescence emission in the 670 nm range. Furthermore, to validate the bio-labeling properties of Fe-CDs, the fluorescence images of a thin film of Fe-CDs (prepared by evaporating dilute aqueous solution on the clean glass coverslip) were examined via a red band-pass filter of 568 nm (Fig. S4b). Furthermore, we investigated the photobleaching of Fe-CDs with one hour of continuous irradiation at 568 nm excitation light. Thereby, the excellent photostability expanded Fe-CDs application as a possible biological labeling agent. NIR labeling has the advantage of deep penetration ability into biological tissues more effectively than visible light. Similarly, the photoluminescence property of N-CDs was evaluated by UV–vis and fluorescence spectroscopy. The UV–vis absorption spectrum of N-CDs showed strong characteristic bands at 250 and 280 nm (Fig. S5a). These bands indicate the π–π * and n–π * transitions, attributed to the sp2 aromatic CC bond connected with CO and CN functional groups. Similarly, the N-CDs can be excited with 365 nm wavelength and provide emission at 456 nm (Fig. S5b). The photoluminescence (PL) of N-CDs exhibited a strong blue fluorescent emission. Altogether, these results confirmed that Fe and N species are successfully doped into the carbon framework of carbon dots.

The chemical coordination environment of Fe-CDs was investigated for both Fe and N, K-edge through characteristic X-ray absorption spectroscopic (near-edge structure (XANES) and extended fine structure (EXAFS)) analyses. We compared our Fe-CDs nanozyme with Fe foil, FeO, Fe2O3, and Heme (FePc; iron phthalocyanine) as reference samples. As given in Fig. 2a, the XANES curves of Fe K-edge in Fe-CDs-nanozyme exhibited near-edge absorption energy with the values ranging between Fe foil and FePc. The spatially local symmetric characteristic peak position at 7113 eV in the XANES data validated the material carried planar Fe-N4 structure as a single atom supported framework. The N K-edge in the EXAFS spectrum of Fe–CDs exhibited one small peak at 398.8 eV and two prominent peaks at 400 and 405.8 eV (Fig. 2b). The small peak at 398.8 eV can be attributed to the aza-bridge in FePc identical to the energy position of the pyridinic state. The peak at 400 eV conferred the equivalent position with the Fe–N bonding in heme structures in FeN4 nanozyme [58]. However, the broad peak at 405.8 eV could be assigned to pyrrolic or other nitrogen states bonded with the Fe atom, which would shift or weaken the C-N (C-Nσ*) bond energy [59]. Fourier-transformed (FT) k3-weighted EXAFS profile at Fe for Fe-CDs only detected one strong peak around 1.5 Å (Fig. 2c), ascribed to the Fe-N4 coordination at the first shell scattering paths [60]. In contrast, a tiny peak was detected for Fe–Fe at 2.2 Å, shown in the gray shaded area in Fig. 2c.

Next, we explored the wavelet transform (WT) analysis of Fe K-edge EXAFS oscillations for the spatial resolution of both k and R spaces. Excitingly, counterplots of Fe-CDs and FePc displayed only intensity maxima at 5 Å−1, while the Fe-foil showed peak intensity at 8 Å−1, as shown in Fig. 2d. Therefore, according to FT- and WT-EXAFS analyses, the Fe atoms were atomically dispersed in Fe-CDs identical to the active center of a natural enzyme (Heme) with Fe-N4 coordination. Fig. 2e provided the k2-weighted EXAFS oscillations spectra to analyze the structural similarity between the iron coordination in our prepared Fe-CDs nanozyme with reference samples. The Fe-CDs nanozyme shown in red exhibited remarkably similar amplitudes and periods to the EXAFS of reference FePc (pink) with minor fluctuations. Moreover, quantitative analysis of the chemical environment through EXAFS fits the typical N-coordinated Fe single atomic moieties in Fig. 2f, g; details are given in Table S1. The shoulder peak at 2.2 Å, assigned as FeFe scattering paths, was well reported in other FTEXAFS studies [61]. The coordination number of the Fe atom found in the Fe–N structure of Fe-CDs was nearly four, and the mean bond length was about 1.9 Å (Fig. 2f, inset). Thus, the Fe atom is atomically distributed as the Fe-N4 co-doped in the hierarchical carbon matrix of the Fe-CDs nanozyme. These data also support that the Fe-N4 bonding in Fe-CDs nanozyme is homologous with naturally occurring heme pocket of the enzymes.

The multiple enzyme-mimicking properties of N-CDs and Fe-CDs nanozymes were analyzed with typical substrates in physiological conditions. At first, OXD-like activity was explored, in which oxygen is converted into water employing hydrogen (H) donor ( Fig. 3a). In a typical OXD-like catalytic reaction, the H donor role is tested by 3,3′,5,5′-tetramethylbenzidine (TMB) with a blue product of oxidized TMB (TMBox) that exhibit characteristic absorbance at 652 nm (Fig. S6a). Both Fe-CDs and N-CDs showed the capability to oxidize TMB in pH and temperature-dependent manner, as provided in Fig. S6b and c. In our experiments, the maximum OXD-like activities of Fe-CDs and N-CDs were recorded at pH values (4.5) and 40 °C for temperature. Moreover, in a concentration-dependent mode of TMB substrate, the obtained curves of Fe-CDs and N-CDs complied with the typical Michaelis–Menten equation confirming the OXD-like behavior (Fig. 3a). The Fe-CDs showed higher catalytic efficiency than N-CDs under identical conditions, as mentioned in their corresponding K M (Michaelis constant) and V max (maximal reaction velocity) values in Table S2. Therefore, the finding of OXD-like activity strongly favors the dominant catalytic efficacy of Fe-doped over N-doped CDs.

POD is a natural enzyme that catalyzes H2O2 to H2O and generates free radicals, as given in the equation in Fig. 3b. TMB acts as a chromogenic substrate in the presence of Fe-CDs/N-CDs that catalyzed H2O2 into H2O, and TMB is converted into an oxidized blue-colored TMB (TMBox) product with characteristic absorbance at 652 nm, as shown in Fig. S7a. There was a remarkable time-dependent augmentation after adding the Fe-CDs/N-CDs in TMB with varying amounts of H2O2 in an aqueous solution, as given in Fig. 3b. The reaction speed was markedly accelerated with different concentrations of H2O2 for both Fe-CDs and N-CDs nanozymes. We also optimized the conditions for Fe-CDs/N-CDs as POD-mimic nanozyme in the varying pH (pH 1.5–10.5) and temperature values, as shown in Fig. S7b and c, respectively. The optimal POD-like activities were achieved at pH 4.5 and a temperature of 37 °C. The obtained curves for varying concentrations of H2O2 and TMB substrates followed the characteristic Michaelis-Menten kinetics given in Figs. 3b and S8, respectively. Fe-CDs showed the most extraordinary POD-like activity level, as indicated with K M and V max values in Table S3. Fe-CDs demonstrated 2.4 and 3.6-fold higher V max for TMB and H2O2, respectively, compared to N-CDs. These data dominantly support the statement that Fe-doping is vital for H2O2 substrate reduction.

SOD-antioxidant catalyzes the dismutation reaction of O2 • into O2 and H2O2 and regulates the intracellular ROS. Hence, SOD mimetics might be a beneficial therapeutic agent against oxidative stress-induced diseases [29]. Typically, the xanthine reacts with xanthine oxidase (XOD) to generate superoxide that reacts with tetrazolium salt (WST-1) and reduces into chromogenic formazan (440 nm absorption) [62]. As shown in Fig. 3c, under physiological conditions (pH 7.4 at room temperature), formazan production decreased with the increase in Fe-CDs nanozyme concentration indicated vital SOD-like activity of Fe-CDs compared with N-CDs. The electron spin resonance spectroscopy (ESR) analysis was used to further validate the SOD-like activity of nanozymes. Fe-CDs-nanozyme effective quenching of superoxide radicals (O2 • ) compared with N-CDs in the identical SOD conditions (Fig. S9). The triplet (1:1:1) signal formation of 5,5-Dimethyl-1-pyrroline N-oxide (DMPO)-O2 • adduct might be the open ring products of DMPO attacked by O2 • .

CAT mimics have been shown to catalyze the degradation of H2O2 molecules into H2O and O2, which helps regulate the H2O2 accumulation and protect cells from oxidative stress [63]. Next, we checked the CAT-like activity of N-CDs and Fe-CDs under physiological conditions (pH 7.4 at room temperature). As shown clearly in Fig. S10, the Fe-CDs, and N-CDs-nanozymes significantly induced O2 generation. Interestingly, in our system, the tendency of CAT-like activity of N-CDs was remarkably enhanced than the Fe-CDs. The decomposition of H2O2 catalyzed by Fe-CDs and N-CDs curves also compiled Michaelis-Menten kinetics, as given in Fig. 3d. The K M and V max values of Fe-CDs nanozyme were about 2-fold lower than N-CDs presented in Table S4. Likewise, the V max value of Fe-CDs was about 2.1 times lower than that of N-CDs, which implies the more efficient H2O2 decomposition by N-CDs than Fe-CDs.

Like POD, the GPx is a member of the POD enzyme family that plays an essential role in sustaining H2O2. GPx-like system utilizes the reductant provided by the cellular tripeptide glutathione (GSH) to catalyze the H2O2 to H2O and transform it into oxidized glutathione (GSSG). This system was accompanied by GSSG reduction to GSH with the help of the GSH reductase (GR) enzyme and coenzyme nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide phosphate (NADPH) [64]. Thus, the GPx-like activity of Fe-CDs and N-CDs nanozymes was determined by real-time spectrophotometric monitoring of a decrease in NADPH absorption level at 340 nm, as presented in Fig. 3e. The rapid reduction in absorbance of NADPH with time indicates potential GPx-mimic activity in the presence of Fe-CDs. However, the N-CDs provided a non-significant change in absorbance at identical conditions, which indicated that N-CDs lack significant GPx-like activity. The GPx-like catalytic property of Fe-CDs nanozyme was further evaluated by steady-state Michaelis-Menten kinetics with H2O2 as the substrates (Fig. S11). The obtained K M and V max values of the Fe-CDs nanozyme was 4.52 mM and 81.96 µM/min, respectively (Table S5).

Furthermore, Fe-CDs nanozyme could reproduce the TPx-mimic behavior to catalyze thiol-containing small molecules other than GSH, such as cysteine-assisted reduction of H2O2 into H2O. In a standard TPx assay, we varied the H2O2 concentration against the DTNB (5,5′-dithiobis (2-nitrobenzoic acid) as a chromogenic indicator. The absorption at 412 nm and DTNB color changes were markedly declined in the Fe-CDs system with increasing H2O2 concentration (Fig. S12a and b). Similar to the GPx-like activity, the N-CDs showed marginal levels of TPx-like activity with the slightest change in absorbance of the DTNB probe (Fig. S12c and S12d). The TPx-mimic activation pathway can be an alternative to deregulate GSH homeostasis under oxidatively stressed cancer cells [65].

Collectively, our results showed that under physiological conditions, Fe-CDs and N-CDs are involved in the following interrelations (Fig. 3f). The nanozymes exhibited multienzyme-mimic activities (SOD, CAT, GPx, and TPx-like) in physiological pH, while OXD and POD-like activities were within an acidic environment. Likewise, oxygen reduction reaction (ORR), the OXD, and POD-like activities can catalyze oxygen reduction. Contrarily, CAT and SOD-like reactions run in reversible directions to induce oxygen evolution reactions (OER). Hence, following the literature, Fe and N doping in our developed nanozymes system play an essential role in modulating the enzyme activities with ORR and OER responses [66,67]. However, the unique addition of GPx and TPx-like properties of Fe-CDs nanozyme could further prompt the overall enzymatic reactions to modulate the highly reserved cancer cell hemostasis.

We further validated the generation of by-products in enzymatic catalyzed reactions via fluorescence and ESR techniques. Preliminary, we evaluated the nanozymes dependent formation of •OH radicals using terephthalic acid (TA) as a trapping probe. The TA molecule is a selective fluorescent trapping probe used for the detection of •OH radical in an aqueous solution[68]. We detected the higher fluorescent signal of catalyzed TA after Fe-CDs/N-CDs, suggesting more •OH production (Fig. S13a and b). Compared with other control groups, the mixture containing Fe-CDs or N-CDs, H2O2, and TA provided a remarkably enhanced fluorescence signal at 430 nm. Thus, the Fe-CDs/N-CDs exhibit significantly high degradation efficiency of H2O2 to generate •OH radicals, which could be attributed to the POD-like activity.

Subsequently, we detected the •OH and O2 • radicals using the ESR technique, which is usually considered a preferable form to report the ROS existence. We utilized the DMPO as a trapping probe to demonstrate the formation of radicals (•OH and O2 • ), as shown in Fig. 3g and h, respectively. As shown in Fig. 3g, the Fe-CDs and N-CDs nanozymes significantly gave a four-line spectrum in a 1:2:2:1 quartet pattern. According to •OH-induction activity, the signal intensity of •OH was notably enhanced after treating Fe-CDs and N-CDs nanozymes. The system having Fe-CDs/N-CDs and H2O2 efficiently generated the •OH radicals, while no evident signal was detected from other groups. Similarly, no O2 • signal was detected in the absence of nanozymes under identical conditions, as depicted in Fig. 3h. Likewise, the ESR spectra of O2 • provided six characteristic sharp and distinct peaks of DMPO-O2 • adduct in the Fe-CDs/N-CDs+H2O2 +DMPO system. Thus, the nanozymes had a tremendous capacity to generate O2 • radicals.

Meanwhile, the in vitro ROS regulation ability of Fe-CDs/N-CDs in the absence and presence of TMB was demonstrated by ESR. There were evident changes in Fe-CDs/TMB system with varying times (Fig. S14a). The ESR analysis of the Fe-CDs exhibited the availability of oxygen vacancies owing to the unpaired electrons. These results provided evidence of ROS generation through the metal doping effect with available oxygen vacancy, which corresponds to electron spin with change in time. Whereas the effect of pH on ROS generation by Fe-CDs/N-CDs in the presence of TMB was also monitored. As shown in Fig. S14b, ESR provided visible and dense peaks at pH 4.5 that indicated the substantial properties of Fe-CDs and N-CDs, which revealed the OXD-like mimics. Collectively, these results validated the potential of our catalytic nanozymes to regulate the O2 • and •OH radicals. Based on these findings, we believe that both Fe and N doping in the C-graphitic structure is required to accomplish the enzyme-mimic features. Indeed, metal doping can boost the enzyme-like properties of nanozymes that can regulate ROS generation, which holds a broad-spectrum potential in GBM therapy.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vitro cellular distribution and anti-tumor activity of Fe-CDs nanozyme
ce:section.ce:para: Effective ROS manipulation has a long-standing approach in cancer chemotherapy. However, the imbalance of ROS can also impair the normal cell function; thus, effective ROS modulation is critical for its anti-tumor activity. The feasibility and efficacy of Fe-CDs nanozyme for tumor catalytic therapy were investigated in human GBM cell lines (U87MG and U251-TR). Since carbon dots enter the cell through endocytosis and are localized in the lysosome, which imparts an essential function to cytotoxicity [69]. Notably, the POD and OXD-like activities of Fe-CDs nanozyme are pH-dependent, which corresponds to the precise localization of nanozymes within the GBM cells to initiate the lysosomal targeting pathway. As given in the electron micrographs ( Fig. 4a), the Fe-CDs nanozyme were internalized in U87MG and U251-TR and localized within lysosomes that featured as red arrowed marked mono-layered phospholipid membrane organelles. The endocytosed nanozymes were later on guided into the lysosomal acidic compartments. Notably, the intrinsic environment of lysosomes with pH 4.5 − 5.0 was ideal for initiating the POD-like activity by Fe-CDs nanozyme. We also tracked the intracellular localization of Fe-CDs by fluorescence imaging in U87MG cells (Fig. 4b) and U251-TR cells (Fig. S15). Briefly, red-emissive Fe-CDs@Ang were co-loaded with Alexa Fluor 488 conjugate for lysosomal marker visualization. As clearly shown in fluorescence imaging, the Fe-CDs@Ang nanozymes were colocalized with late endosome/lysosomes, which was beneficial to improving the oxidative capacity of the Fe-CDs nanozyme. The evident colocalization of nanozyme within a highly acidic microenvironment (pH 4–5) would ease the OXD and POD-like activities for tumor catalytic therapy to kill GBM cells [70].

We then investigated the in vitro targeting performance of Fe-CDs@Ang and un-modified nanozymes (Fe-CDs) by flow cytometric analysis. As shown in Fig. 4c, angiopep-2 functionalized Fe-CDs nanozyme exhibited significantly higher cellular uptake in U87MG cells than un-modified counterparts. We then determined the cytotoxicity of various healthy and cancer cell lines exposed to the Fe-CDs@Ang. As given in Fig. 4d, a tremendous decrease in viability of U251-TR cells emerged in the varying doses of nanozyme with an IC50 value of 22.15 µg/mL (complementary details given in Table S6). Compared to other tumor cell lines (U87MG, LNZ-308, HeLA), the Fe-CDs@Ang nanozyme showed significantly enhanced anti-tumor activity for U251-TR followed by U87MG under the same conditions. Interestingly, the Fe-CDs@Ang nanozymes did not cause significant toxicity in healthy cell lines (microglia and human hepatocyte cell line (LO2), thus, possessing excellent biocompatibility.

To validate our idea, intracellular ROS probe dichlorofluorescein diacetate (H2DCFDA) and specific ROS scavenger N-acetyl-L-cysteine (NAC) antagonizes used to manipulate ROS levels. We quantified the intracellular ROS levels via flow cytometry within targeted cell lines (U251-TR and U87MG) after treatment with nanozymes (N-CDs, Fe-CDs, and Fe-CDs@Ang). As shown in Fig. 4e, and f, a 102 to 103-folds higher ROS signal of H2DCFDA staining was detected with Fe-CDs@Ang, than N-CDs and Fe-CDs in U251-TR and U87MG cell lines. Thus, an intense and proper ROS manipulation was initiated by the Fe-CDs@Ang in U251-TR and U87MG cell lines. Moreover, we also detected the time-dependent ROS generation via H2DCFDA in the presence of intrinsic fluorescence of carbon dots channels (excitation at 568 nm) in U251-TR cells. The time-dependent ROS production showed upregulated ROS levels within 12 h after Fe-CDs nanozyme treatment (Fig. S16).

Next, we evaluated whether Fe-CDs@Ang is actually responsible for the elevated ROS-mediated cytotoxicity in cell lines. The U87MG cells were preincubated with the ROS scavenger (NAC), followed by Fe-CDs@Ang treatment. Quantitatively estimated ROS signal showed a rapid elevation in ROS levels after Fe-CDs@Ang nanozyme treatment (Fig. S17a). Meanwhile, the time-dependent fluorescence signal was upregulated in 30 min and steadily increased up to 3 h, as shown in Fig. S17b. The data further ensured the direct relation in ROS formation with Fe-CDs@Ang nanozyme treatment time. We additionally measured the ROS elevation effect on the cellular fate of U251-TR cell lines. The NAC significantly inhibited the nanozyme-induced reduction of the cell numbers in U251-TR (Fig. 4g). Together, these results suggested that upregulated ROS caused nanozyme-mediated cytotoxicity in GBM cells.

We then employed the quantitative lactase dehydrogenase (LDH) assay and fluorescence microscopy for mitochondrial membrane potential (MMP) to characterize the consequences of Fe-CDs@Ang on cell membranes and mitochondria. The release of LDH from the cytosol is considered an early sign of cell membrane damage. Fig. 4h showed a 1.8-fold higher LDH release in the Fe-CDs@Ang treated cell than in PBS control. Considering the massive ROS generation, we evaluated the effect of Fe-CDs@Ang nanozyme treatment on mitochondrial membrane potential (∆Ψ m). The fluorescence imaging of JC-1 dye showed the excessive hyperpolarized/depolarized mitochondria (green color) after N-CDs, Fe-CDs, and Fe-CDs@Ang treatment (Fig. 4i). In the PBS control group, intact ∆Ψm (red color) was dominant. These results strongly support the idea that both N and Fe doping triggers the apoptotic cascade in the U251-TR cells to form JC-1 monomers in damaged cells with low ∆Ψ m. Mitochondrial dysfunction was responsible for releasing apoptotic markers that can initiate autophagy/apoptosis [71]. We further investigated the mitochondrial ROS generation by MitoSox Red staining (selective mitochondrial ROS indicator) [72]. We found that Fe-CDs@Ang treatment elevated MitoSox Red intensity (Fig. S18), which attenuated the mitochondrial dysfunction in U87MG cells. Hence, the results strongly evident the efficient intracellular uptake with precise localization, noticeable ROS up-regulation, and impressive mitochondria dysfunction by Fe-CDs@Ang enable it as an effective artificial enzyme. Once inside the acidic milieu of lysosomes, they might cause GBM cell death by oxygen consumption that leads to hypoxia modulation and synergistic upregulation of the ROS levels.

After confirming the ROS-based cascade hypothesis, we evaluated the nanozymes-based molecular mechanism for GBM therapy. Fig. 5a represents a schematic illustration of a plausible mechanism for Fe-CDs nanozymes to selectively upregulate lysosome-mediated autophagy in drug-resistant GBM cells. It is well-known that metal-based nanoparticles activate various autophagy pathways to initiate the death of cancer cells [73]. We detected the osmotic swelling of the mitochondrial matrix after the Fe-CDs exposure caused outer membrane permeabilization of mitochondria in U87MG and U251-TR cells (Fig. 5b). The plenty of Fe-CDs@Ang (red arrows) was colocalized in autophagosomes (fussed activated lysosome and phagosome), while their released ROS directly deformed the mitochondrial morphology (green arrows). Interestingly, we detected that the drug-resistant U251-TR cells produced more autophagosomes (white bubble-like structure) than U87MG cells (Fig. S19a). Though, after 24 h treatment, both cell lines exhibited the phenomena of prominent vacuolization that was enough to initiate an apoptotic flux. In contrast, no significant damage to mitochondria was detected in the untreated GBM cell lines (Fig. S19b). The morphological features of the macro-sized autophagy vacuoles (autophagosomes shown with red arrows) were further explored in BioTEM (Fig. 5c), which was evidently indicated by a double-membraned autophagosome enveloped with precisely damaged mitochondria. The vacuoles have engulfed plenty of the damaged mitochondria to form autophagosomes/autolysosomes that ultimately shed off from the cells to initiate apoptosis.

Considering the ROS upregulation signal with damaged mitochondria can ultimately lead to cell cycle arrest. The overall cytotoxic effect of Fe-CDs nanozymes was quantified by Flow cytometry in U251-TR cells. Fig. 5d exhibited the fluorescein-annexin V and propidium iodide (PI) double stain assay for U251-TR cells toxicity by nanozymes mainly caused by apoptosis/necrosis. Compared to the untreated control group, N-CDs, Fe-CDs, and Fe-CDs@Ang groups showed an evident increase in late-apoptosis/necrosis-induced cell death activation. The overall apoptotic/necrotic cells percentage of the Fe-CDs@Ang group was significantly higher, 71.34%, than the Fe-CDs (27.59%) and N-CDs (17.33%) groups. These results demonstrated that angiopep-2 functionalized nanozyme possess adequate cell apoptosis/necrosis capacity.

Furthermore, we confirmed the expressions of proteins linked to apoptotic/necrotic cell death in U251-TR and U87MG cells via the immunoblot technique. The Fe-CDs@Ang nanozyme treatment in a time-dependent manner was studied for hypoxia-inducible factor 1 (HIF-1α), B-cell lymphoma 2 (Bcl-2), Cyclophilin A (CypA) markers in U87MG and U251-TR cells. The elevated CypA release from the permeable cell membrane is considered an early-stage necrosis flux initiation in targeted cells [74]. Results in Fig. 5e showed interesting downregulation of the anti-apoptotic marker (Bcl-2), sequent to the upregulated pro-apoptotic (CypA) marker in U87MG and U251-TR cells with the increasing treatment time. Moreover, we emphasized the critical role of ROS in the induction of apoptotic marker expression. We detected the significant downregulation of CypA release in ROS inhibitor (NAC) pre-treated U87MG and U251-TR cell lines (Fig. S20). The HIF-1α is a crucial regulator of the transcriptional response to acute and chronic hypoxia [75]. After treatment with Fe-CDs@Ang, the expression level of HIF-1α was markedly down-regulated with time. As for the explanation of these results, they were firstly localized in the early endosome (pH ~ 6.5) during the process of Fe-CDs@Ang nanozymes being internalized into tumor cells [76]. In this period, the nanozyme typically exhibits the cascade reaction of SOD and CAT-like activities. Since the tumor cells, especially drug-resistant tumor cells are typically along with an elevated level of superoxide radicals [77], the cascade reaction of Fe-CDs@Ang nanozymes could efficiently catalyze the superoxide radicals to oxygen to relieve the hypoxia (Scheme 1). In addition, the Fe-CDs also exhibited GPx and TPx-like activities in this stage, which consumed GSH to make tumor cells more sensitive to the ROS changes. Hence nanozyme treatment induces many important changes in the TME redox hemostasis, rather than only transient tumors oxygenation.

As per confirmation of ROS-mediated apoptotic effect that could induce autophagy was also interpreted in U87MG and U251-TR cell lines. The immunoblot study examined the standard autophagic markers expression such as LC3-I/LC3-II, p62 or sequestosome 1 (SQSTM1), and Beclin 1 [78]. The molecular data corresponding to expression levels of LC3 I/II, p62, and Beclin1 in U87MG and U251-TR cells are presented in Fig. 5f. The LC3-II/I ratio was higher in Fe-CDs@Ang treated U87MG and U251-TR cells than that in other groups but relatively lower in the cells pretreated with NAC. The results suggest that ROS depletion directly impacts the cell fate that inhibits the autophagosome process in our case. Similarly, the expression of preferential degrading autophagy substrate (ubiquitinated-like p62) was elevated, which conferred the nanozyme-mediated autophagy impairment (Fig. 5f). In addition, the Beclin-1 (autophagy-initiated protein) level was almost unchanged in U87MG cells while downregulated in U251-TR cells. Motivated by the finding of these molecular responses, we can speculate that the nanozyme treatment either upregulated the autophagosome formation or autophagosome-lysosome fusion process, particularly in U251-TR cell lines.

ce:section.ce:section-title: BBB penetration study using Fe-CDs@Ang nanozyme
ce:section.ce:para: Next, we evaluated the BBB penetration for in vitro and in vivo models to validate the nanozymes targeting ability for GBM. To measure the BBB penetration of Fe-CDs@Ang nanozyme, an in vitro BBB model was developed as; immortalized mouse brain endothelial cells (bEnd.3) monolayer seeded on the top of the inserted lumen, and human U87MG cells were present on the bottom-top of a membrane within Transwell culture plate (Fig. 5g). CDs were added from the top chamber and penetrated into the bottom chamber. A time-dependent permeation process was studied by analyzing the transport ratio of N-CDs, Fe-CDs, and Fe-CDs@Ang nanozymes across the engineered BBB in vitro model. Penetrability was determined by quantifying the proportion of CDs (transport ratio) penetrating the bottom chamber. The cumulative transport ratio of Fe-CDs@Ang nanozymes was significantly higher than un-modified N-CDs and Fe-CDs under identical conditions (Fig. 5g). Subsequently, we assess the tumor-targeting ability of Fe-CDs@Ang nanozymes for in vivo BBB mice model. The U87MG glioma orthotopically implanted in nude mice were injected with angiopep-2 modified and un-modified Fe-CDs by intravenous (i.v.) tail injection. Compared with un-modified Fe-CDs, we observed significant brain accumulation of Fe-CDs@Ang with sustained bright fluorescence up to 24 h (Fig. 5h), indicating the vital role of angiopep-2 in enabling substantial brain accumulation. For pharmacokinetic studies, Fe-CDs and Fe-CDs@Ang were administered to the tumor-free mice via i.v. tail injection. Fluorescence intensity (excitation at 568 nm) was recorded at different post-injection time intervals. Notably, Fe-CDs@Ang has a prolonged plasma elimination half-life (t 1/2, β) of 3.725 h, while un-modified Fe-CDs were recorded as 1.0 h (Fig. S21). These data proved that angiopep-2 bioconjugation to carbon dots improves the pharmacokinetics of nanozymes in plasma.

Interestingly, the ex vivo study of the organs demonstrated that Fe-CDs@Ang was effectively taken up and accumulated in the brain tumor compared to other organs (Fig. 5i). The intrinsic fluorescence of Fe-CDs@Ang was significantly colocalized in the brain compared to the N-CDs and Fe-CDs. This colocalization of fluorescence intensities suggested the post-injected Fe-CDs were mainly accumulated within the brain region owing to their smaller size. The organ biodistribution of Fe-CDs@Ang was quantified using fluorometric measurement of intrinsic fluorescence of CDs in mice (Fig. 5j). The ex-vivo biodistribution proved that brain accumulation was around 4.5% of the injected dosage/gram (% ID/g) of tissue, significantly higher (p < 0.001) than N-CDs and Fe-CDs. Thereby, the raised values of % ID/g of nanozymes in the kidney and liver suggested that the nanozymes might follow these excretory pathways.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vivo anti-tumor effect of Fe-CDs@Ang nanozyme
ce:section.ce:para: The anti-tumor efficacy of Fe-CDs@Ang was further explored in mice bearing orthotopic U87MG-Luc and U251-TR tumors subjected to glioma therapy. After 12 days post-implantation, the U87MG-Luc, and U251-TR glioma-bearing nude mice were treated by i.v. tail injection of different nanozymes at every alternate day ( Fig. 6a) before the sample was collected for tissue analysis. Moreover, the bioluminescence imaging was performed on every subsequent day of treatment to screen the tumor development. Among various groups, mice that received Fe-CDs@Ang treatment showed an effective tumor inhibition compared to N-CDs, Fe-CDs, NHS-PEG2k-Mal-Ang, and untreated control group with weak glioma growth inhibition (Fig. 6b).

Moreover, the Fe-CDs@Ang had significantly inhibited the tumor growth, evidenced by the reduced Luceferase-bioluminescence among various treated groups with the relatively lower photon flux in the U87MG tumor model (Fig. 6c). We also analyzed successive anti-tumor effects and survival studies within mice bearing orthotropic U251-TR models. The results showed a significant body weight loss in both U87MG-Luc (Fig. 6d) and U251-TR tumor models (Fig. 6e) in NHS-PEG2k-Mal-Ang and saline control groups. The body weight loss was likely due to aggressive GBM progression and invasion into the healthy CNS. The Fe-CDs@Ang treated animals showed a marked increase in survival. The median survival time was 56 and 52 days for U87MG (Fig. 6f) and U251-TR (Fig. 6g) bearing mice models, respectively. In contrast, the N-CDs (38 days for U251-TR) and Fe-CDs (42 days for U251-TR) showed a much shorter survival time than the Fe-CDs@Ang group. Besides, the survival of mice treated with NHS-PEG2k-Mal-Ang (23 days) and saline control (20 days) was significantly low (Fig. 6f, and g).

We then performed tumor tissue immunohistochemistry (IHC) and histology of all the treated and untreated groups. As shown in Fig. 6h, the histology staining demonstrated that massive necrosis and apoptosis occurred in GBM tissues after being treated with Fe-CDs@Ang, Fe-CDs, and N-CDs nanozymes than control groups (PBS and Ang-Mal-PEG2k-NHS). Meanwhile, a similar trend in the number of apoptotic tumor cells was found in control groups, presented via TUNEL staining and cleaved caspase-3 (CC3) assay within U251-TR models (Fig. 6h). Similar conclusions were drawn for H&E, CC3, and TUNEL assays of U87MG tumor tissue slices (Fig. S22). We also found autophagy markers (LC3 and p62) in both U251-TR (Fig. 6i) and U87MG (Fig. S22) slices from mice. IHC data showed that the LC3 and p62 positive dots were evident in Fe-CDs@Ang, Fe-CDs, and N-CDs compared with control groups. The doted structure in IHC staining showed the presence of the autophagosomes or fussed lysosomes positive staining. Amazingly, the expressions of autophagy markers were dominant in Fe-CDs@Ang treated groups compared with those of control and unmodified peptide Fe-CDs groups in U251-TR and U87MG brain tumors.

In addition, in vivo toxicity of Fe-CDs, and N-CDs nanozyme were systematically investigated to ensure their safe bioapplications. First, the H&E staining assay of major organs, including liver, heart, kidney, lung, spleen, and normal brain, proved the high histocompatibility of Fe-CDs@Ang nanozymes (Fig. S23). Furthermore, we evaluated the liver status by determining the levels of alanine transaminase (ALT), aspartate transaminase (AST), alkaline phosphatase (ALP), whilst kidney function was examined by measuring blood urea nitrogen (BUN) and creatinine (CREA) markers. A negligible difference was observed between N-CDs, Fe-CDs, and Fe-CDs@Ang nanozymes-treated and control groups (Fig. S24), proving that nanozymes exhibit negligible toxicity to the liver and kidney. Moreover, a significant amount of Fe content via Fe-CDs fluorescence spectrophotometric analysis was detected to be excreted from the mice body through urine and faces samples 48 h after administration (Fig. S25). Collectively, our experimental results suggest that Fe-CDs@Ang nanozymes induce a risk-free and effective catalytic anticancer therapy in mice with orthotopic brain tumors.

This study developed a novel biomimetic nanozyme based on green and drug-free nanomedicine strategies to efficiently target brain tumor cells. Owing to the Fe-N4 active enzyme center, the developed nanozyme provides multifunctionality endowed in the carbon matrix. The Fe-CDs nanozyme has the potential to generate six innate enzyme-like activities (OXD, POD, SOD, CAT, GPX, and TPx) that mimic the intracellular antioxidant defense system for ROS-mediated killing in drug-resistant GBM cells. The in vitro and in vivo experiments verified the role of Fe-CDs@Ang nanozymes in exceptional biocompatibility, considerable BBB penetration, high tumor accumulation/targetability, and autophagy triggered cell death responses. Thus, drug-free nanomedicine provides excellent benefits to achieving high efficacy and minimal toxicity in malignant GBM therapy. Collectively, multifunctional Fe-CDs nanozyme is acknowledged as a powerful toolkit for the specialized application in the treatment of drug-resistant GBM.

All of the chemicals and reagents used in this study were of analytical quality and obtained from the supplier specified otherwise mentioned. The cancer cell culture medium and chemicals were purchased from HyCloneTM, GE Healthcare Bio-Sciences, Pittsburgh, (USA). Nest Biotechnologies Co., Ltd (Wuxi, Jiangsu), China, provided all the flasks used in cell culture tests. Cell lines used in this experiment were provided by the Chinese Academy of Sciences (CAS), Shanghai, China. The luciferase gene was transfected in U87MG and TMZ resistance U251-TR cells from iCell Biosience Inc., Shanghai, China. Polycarbonate-coated Transwell upper chambers (24 mm; 0.4 µm pore size) were purchased from Corning (NY, USA). 3-[4, 5-dimethylthiazol-2-yl]-3, 5-diphenyltetrazolium bromide solution (MTT), Annexin V and PI, BCA Protein, and JC-1 membrane potential assay kits were obtained from Beyotime (China). Triton X-100, 4′,6′-diamidino-2-phenylidole (DAPI), and TUNEL Apoptosis Assay Kit were procured from Roche Applied Science. Glutaraldehyde (50%, w/v), paraformaldehyde, 2′,7′-dichlorofluorescein diacetate (H2DCF-DA), and 5,5–dimethyl-1-pyrroline N-oxide (DMPO) were obtained from Sigma-Aldrich. BD Biosciences supplied the poly-L-lysine-treated coverslips. Mouse anti-Lamp1 monoclonal antibody, cyclophilin A (CypA), and goat anti-mouse IgG1 conjugated with Alexa-488 were purchased from Sino Biological (China). Other primary antibodies (LC3, p62, and Beclin 1) were purchased from Proteintech, China. Other reagents are mentioned in the experimental section with their respective labels. Antibodies for HiF-1α and β-actin were obtained from Abcam (Cambridge, UK). ImageJ software was used for the data quantification. The water used in the experiments was deionized with a Milli-Q water purification system (Merck, Germany), with a conductivity of −18mῼ/cm2. Angiopep-2 (TFFYGGSRGKRNNFKTEEYC) was acquired from China Peptide Co., Ltd. (Suzhou, China). Maleimide PEG Succinimidyl Carboxymethyl Ester (NHS-PEG2000-MAL) was purchased from Ponsure Biological (Shanghai, China). The cell culture supplements (fetal bovine serum (FBS), non-essential amino acids, penicillin-streptomycin, and 0.25% (w/v) trypsin solution) were obtained from Gibco BRL (Gaithersburg, MD, USA).

The hydrodynamic size distribution was determined by a dynamic light scattering (Nano ZS, Malvern) instrument. The absorbance spectra of the suspension were measured on a multifunction microplate reader (Devices/13x). Cell and tissue slices were examined using laser scanning confocal microscopy and collected using a laser scanning confocal microscope (Zeiss 880). The morphology of the N-CDs and Fe-CDs was investigated using the high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM) from JEM-2100 (JEOL, Tokyo, Japan) with 200 kV acceleration voltage. The lacey carbon-coated copper grids were used for HR-TEM analysis. BioTEM was detected using a low acceleration voltage of 80 kV using a TEM microscope (JEM-2100, JEOL, Tokyo, Japan). The fluorescent properties of the prepared nanoparticles were characterized on an F-7000 fluorescence spectrometer (Horiba High-Technologies Corporation, Tokyo, Japan). The radicals (•OH and O2 •–) determination process was measured by an ESR spectrometer (Bruker EMX plus, Germany). Elemental identification was carried out utilizing Thermo Scientific's Energy-dispersive X-ray fluorescence (EDXRF), having a 120 kV voltage. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was analyzed using a Thermo Fisher Scientific ESCALAB 250 spectrometer. A (hʋ 1486.6 eV) monochromatic Al Kα radiation source was used to explore the surface chemical characteristics. The high-resolution C1s, N1s, O1s, and Fe 4f core levels convoluted XPS spectra were measured at a pass energy of 29.35 eV with a take-off angle of 90°. Transfected cells and nanozyme (enzymatic) assays were quantitatively measured using a microplate reader (Molecular Devices, USA). The cellular ROS and apoptosis assay were achieved through a flow cytometer (BD FACS Calibur, San Jose) and CytoFLEX Platform (Beckman Coulter, Inc). A dual-functional bioluminescent and fluorescent Lumina IVIS III system (IVIS, Lumina III; Caliper, MA, USA) carried out all the in vivo and ex vivo studies.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of iron doped carbon dots (Fe-CDs)
ce:section.ce:para: In a green synthesis procedure, the Fe-CDs were biosynthesized using pure bougainvillea plant leaves as a carbon precursor [79]. Briefly, leaves were processed by washing with water, dried at room temperature naturally, and then chopped using a hand blender. Subsequently, ∼10 g of chopped dry leaves were mixed in 100 mL of a binary solution of ethanol-water [1:1, v/v] to get a soluble green C and N product. The extract was then allowed to be mixed with iron (II) chloride (10% w/v) and subsequently sonicated for another 10 min to get a homogenized mixture. Under rotating conditions, the obtained homogenous mixture was subjected to 90% power in a domestic microwave oven (700 W power) to get the carbonized product. The final product was obtained as a deep dark brown thick liquid containing multiple size Fe-CDs particles and debris. Then the mixture was sonicated, followed by high-speed centrifugation at 6∼10k rpm for 30 min, and the supernatant was collected. The supernatant solution contained small dots with up to< 15 nm particle size, while the pallet containing large-sized bulk micro-sized particles and reaction by-products were discarded. Finally, the collected supernatant solution was transferred to a petri dish and placed on a water bath to get dried, then the powder of red-emissive Fe-CDs was collected for further use.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Conjugation of Angiopep-2 (Ang) with CDs surfaces
ce:section.ce:para: The conjugation of Fe-CDs with Ang was prepared by method elsewhere [80] with minor modification. To conjugate Ang-CDs, 100 μL of the Fe-CDs pellets were added with NHS-PEG2k-Mal-Ang (50 µg) for 30 min in PBS (pH 7.4). Before that, a conjugation reaction of NHS-PEG2k-Mal to Ang was produced in PBS buffer (with a 1:3 molar ratio) for 12 h at room temperature. The product NHS-PEG2k-Mal-Ang was separated by dialysis procedure (MWCO: 3500) against water for 48 h to remove free or unbound peptides, followed by lyophilization. The micro-BCA assay was applied to obtain the degree of conjugation of Ang to the CD's surface.

The N-CDs were prepared with recipes published elsewhere with minor modifications [81]. Briefly, the citric acid (1.5 g) as a carbon source was mixed with ethylenediamine (550 μL) in 10 mL of water through sonication. The mixture was shifted to a 30 mL poly (tetrafluoroethylene) (Teflon)-lined autoclave tube and allowed to continuously heated at 200 ºC for 6 h. The N-CD product was separated through subsequent centrifugation (10k rpm) for 20 min to remove the unreacted by-products. The supernatant containing N-CDs was then syringe filtered (0.22 µm) and freeze-dried to get the solid product of N-CDs.

The POD activity was evaluated from the recipes mentioned elsewhere [28]. The typical POD-like activity assays were determined in the presence of TMB (as the substrate) and H2O2 in buffer solution (0.1 M HAc-NaAc, pH 4.5). The absorbance of the blue-colored product (at 652 nm for TMB) was measured at a particular reaction time using a microplate reader spectrophotometer. Briefly, a certain quantity of Fe-CDs and N-CDs were added into a buffer solution (1.0 mL) containing 10 μL TMB (0.416 mM) and H2O2 (0.5292 M) to determine the chromogenic reactions. The standard steady-state kinetic tests were conducted at 40 °C in a 1 mL reaction buffer solution. Then, the equal volume of catalyst (Fe-CDs or N-CDs) solution [10 μL of 2.5 mg/mL] was allowed to react in the presence of TMB and H2O2 substrates. In the same way, the reaction kinetic assays of Fe-CDs and N-CDs were recorded by adding the aliquots of 10 μL of H2O2 (30%, v/v) and variable amounts (1, 2, 4, 6, 8, 10, 12.5, 15, 17.5, 20 μL) of TMB solution (in DMSO, 10 mg/mL). Similarly, the POD-like kinetic assays were also achieved by changing the H2O2 concentrations (5, 10, 20, 30, 40, 50, 60, 70, 80, 100, 150, 200 μL) solutions while keeping the TMB (10 μL of 10 mg/mL) and Fe-CDs/N-CDs catalyst (10 μL of 2.5 mg/mL) conditions the same.

The absorbance of each reaction was measured at various reaction times during the process. The characteristic Michaelis–Menten constant (K M) and velocity (V max) were calculated using the saturation curve in Origin Pro 2019b (evaluation version). The POD-like activity of Fe-CD/N-CDs in buffer solutions with pH values ranging from 1.5 to 11 was studied using the same substrate conditions. Similarly, the temperature dependence effect on POD-like activity was achieved in temperatures ranging from 25 °C to 60 °C.

The oxidation of TMB in the mild acidic buffer in the presence of Fe-CDs/N-CDs catalyst produced a blue-colored signal with typical absorbance peaks at 370 and 652 nm. In a standard OXD-like activity assay, a specific amount of catalyst was added into a 1.0 mL buffer solution (pH 4.5) and mixed with aliquots of 10 μL of TMB (0.416 mM). Kinetic measurements of the standard OXD reactions of Fe-CDs/N-CDs catalyst were carried through a microplate reader at 652 nm. The kinetic assays of Fe-CDs/N-CDs catalyst (10 μL of 2.5 mg/mL) with TMB as the substrate were performed by adding different amounts (1, 2, 4, 6, 8, 10, 12.5, 15, 20 μL) of TMB solution (in DMSO, 10 mg/mL) and the varying time of reaction rate was measured. The K M and V max were calculated by using Michaelis- Menten saturation curve in Origin Pro 2019 (evaluation version).

The standard CAT-like activity analyses of Fe-CDs/N-CDs were measured by recording the generated oxygen (O2) by applying an oxygen-sensitive electrode on Multi-Parameter Analyzer (JPSJ-606 L, Leici China) at room temperature. Briefly, aliquots of 0.2 mL N-CDs or Fe-CDs (2.5 mg/mL) were mixed gently in 3.0 mL buffer solution (10 mM, PB buffer, pH 7.4) and then 180 μL H2O2 (30%, v/v) solution was added. The oxygen sensor directly measured the O2 solubility level (unit: mg L−1) at different reaction times. Using H2O2 as the substrate, we evaluated the steady kinetic assays of Fe-CDs or N-CDs catalyst by changing the different volumes (0, 15, 30, 60, 90, 120, 150, 225, 300 μL) of H2O2 (30%, v/v) solution, while keeping the buffer condition the same. The K M and V max were calculated by using Michaelis- Menten saturation curve in Origin Pro 2019 (evaluation version). The generated O2 photos were taken using a mobile phone camera.

The commercially available colorimetric SOD assay kit (S311–10, from Dojindo Molecular Technologies) was used to get the SOD-like activity assays of Fe-CDs and N-CDs at room temperature. The assay was performed as per the instructions of the manufacturer. In regular SOD-like activity, % inhibition of the competitive WST reaction having superoxide through natural SOD enzyme was validated against the Fe-CDs/N-CDs concentrations.

The GPx-like activity of Fe-CDs/N-CDs nanozymes was achieved by using a commercially obtained total GPx assay kit (Beyotime, China). The spectrophotometric assay was performed under the guidance of the manufacturer’s instructions. Briefly, a Kit containing a GSH (final concentration: 8.4 mM) was oxidized to GSSG with H2O2 (10 mM) substrate presence of 100 μg/mL of Fe-CDs/N-CDs catalysts. Then GR (glutathione reductase) coupled reaction with reduced NADPH further facilitates the reduction of GSSG to GSH with vital reducing power. The quantity of NADPH (300 µM) was reduced, which resulted in a drop in absorbance at 340 nm, which was detected using a microplate reader. The K M and V max Michaelis-Menten saturation curve was obtained only for Fe-CDs nanozyme, which showed the GPx-like activity and was determined via Origin Pro 2019 (evaluation version). However, the marginal change in GPx-like character shown in N-CDs did not follow the standard Michaelis-Menten saturation curve.

The TPx-like activity was monitored by recipes published elsewhere [38]. In a typical TPx activity test, using cysteine (2 mM) and Fe-CDs or N-CDs (20 μL of 100 μg mL−1) with varying concentration of H2O2 (0, 0.8, 1.6, 3.2, 6.4, and 9.6 mM) substrate in PBS (pH 7.4). Then every 60 s, the 100 μL of the obtained mixture was further added and mixed with another 100 μL DTNB (5 mM) in methanol solution, which was allowed to react for an additional 3 min before being diluted to 1 mL with PBS (pH 7.4). As the reaction progressed, the yellow color of DTNB was slowly disappeared, and the absorbance at 412 nm was recorded. The control reactions were performed the same except without Fe-CDs, and N-CDs (n = 3 for each group).

The release of CyPA (necrosis) and other apoptotic related proteins (BcL2), hypoxia (HIF-1α), and autophagy-related (LC3-II, I, p62, beclin 1) markers were determined by WB. The culture U87MG and U251-TR cell supernatant were collected after being treated with N-CDs, Fe-CDs, and Fe-CDs@Ang and subsequently centrifuged to remove suspended cells or debris. In contrast, β Actin was run as a reference marker in all immunoblots. SDS-page was used to separate cell lysates, and supernatants were transferred to the PVDF membrane (GE Healthcare Life Sciences, Marlborough, MA, USA) for immunoblotting with the specified antibodies. After that, the membrane blockage was performed by Tris-buffer saline containing 0.1% Tween-20 (v/v) and 5% non-fat containing dry milk (w/v). After the blocking step, membranes were exposed to the above-mentioned primary antibodies or β-Actin at a suitable dilution for 14 h incubation at 4 °C under constant shaking. The triply washed membrane was then incubated with secondary antibodies (Alexa Fluor 550 IgG (H + L) for 2 h. Finally, four times (10 min each) washed membrane was subjected to band density under Amersham Imager 680 System.

All animal handling protocols and experiments were approved by the Medical and Scientific Research Ethics Committee of Henan University School of Medicine (PR China) (HUSOM-2021–196). All protocols of the animal studies conformed to the Guide for the Care and Use of Laboratory Animals. China. For in vivo study, 6–8 weeks old BALB/c nude female mice were ordered from Hunan Silaike Jinda Laboratory (China). Meanwhile, the nude mice were purchased from Charles River Laboratories. To develop an orthotopic U87MG GBM-bearing mouse model with about a 100% success rate, minced GBM tissue was implanted into BALB/c mice's left striatum. Each mouse was injected with 1 × 107 concentration of U87MG-Luc cells suspension containing 50 μL of 0.9% NaCl (w/v) into the flank of the nude BALB/c mouse. The subcutaneous tumor was allowed to grow up to a volume of about 200 mm3, and then tumor tissues were resected. Following that, a 24# trocar and a precisely made propeller were used to implant 2 mg minced U87MG tumor tissue into the left striatum of 3 mm dept with 2 mm lateral to the bregma in the pentobarbital sodium anesthetized (80 mg) animals. The burr-hole was confined with special bone wax (Johnson & Johnson International, Brussels, Belgium), and the scalp was then fastened with tissue glue (3 M Animal Care Products, St Paul, Minnesota, USA). The tumor development was observed by bioluminescent imaging (IVIS, Lumina III; Caliper, MA, USA) performed in 10 min post-anesthetized mice in the presence of luciferase substrate (15 mg/mL D-luciferin potassium in PBS). The U251-TR tumor model was established by the same procedure except for collected cells (10 μL of 5 ×103 cells) in PBS and subsequently injected in BALB/c athymic mice skulls. The skull was then sealed using bone sealing wax, and the skin incision was healed with tissue adhesive glue (VetbondTM, 3 M, Japan).

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vitro BBB model permeation study
ce:section.ce:para: We used the Transwell approach to culture endothelial cells, which mimic BBB. The endothelial cells (bEnd.3) were cultured in a 25 cm2 cell culture flask. After attaining 90% confluency of grown cells, the cells were trypsinized and then subjected to culture in the Transwell membranes (Millicell®) at 100 nm pore size until the cell’s conductivity resistance was above 300 Ω, estimated via epithelial voltammeter (EVOM2, instruments, Sarasota, FL 34240). Furthermore, the membranes were shifted to 24 well plates cultured with various cells depending on further evaluation. We also cultured the U87MG human glioma cells in the lower layer of the cell culture device, and the efficiency of the Fe-CDs and Fe-CDs@Ang across the endothelial cells was evaluated. In this experiment, the BBB penetration ability of Fe-CDs and Fe-CDs@Ang were detected and quantified by CLSM (Zeiss 880).

For the transcytosis study, the media solution in the upper chamber was substituted with a 190 μL of complete fresh media, and then 10 μL N-CDs, Fe-CDs, and Fe-CDs@Ang were added. The transwells were allowed into a stirring incubator (37 °C, 50 rpm). According to specific time (2, 8, 24 h) points, aliquots of 300 μL solution were collected from the basolateral compartment and interchanged with the fresh medium of identical volume. The TEER was measured again at the end of the experiment for the bEnd.3 cell monolayer integrity measurement. Finally, the % transport ratio of CDs penetration was estimated via monitoring the fluorescence intensities of samples using SpectraMax i3x multi-mode microplate reader (Ex/Em = 568/670 nm for Fe-CDs and Ex/Em = 365/456 nm for N-CDs).

To evaluate the in vivo tumor targetability of nanozyme, fluorescent CDs were i.v. injected via tail vein of U87MG glioma orthotopic mice models. At various time points of 0, 2, 4, 8, 12, and 24 h of the post i.v. injection received either group (N-CDs, Fe-CDs, and Fe-CDs@Ang) nanozyme. The mice that received the nanozymes were anesthetized with isoflurane through a nasal cone device during the image acquisition procedure. The dual-mode near-infrared fluorescence imaging system (Lumia III software) obtained fluorescence photos by keeping the Ex/Em at 568/670 nm.

The (i.v.) to tail vein injection of Fe-CDs and Fe-CDs@Ang (50 μL, Fe-CDs = 4 mg/mL) were received by female BALB/c nude mice (n = 5) after 2 weeks post-implantation. After 12 h of injection, the tumor-bearing mice were sacrificed, and the primary organs (kidney, lung, spleen, liver, heart, and solid tumor) were collected and cleaned in PBS. The IVIS Lumina III was used to perform fluorescence imaging under a 568 nm excitation band filter. Then, the organs were homogenized for 10 min in 0.6 mL solution containing 1% Triton X-100 in a homogenizer (IKA T25) at 12,000 rpm before measuring the fluorescence spectrophotometer. All the tissue lysates were incubated in 0.9 mL ethanol at room temperature overnight. Finally, the homogenized samples were subjected to centrifugation at 10k rpm for 15 min; the fluorescence signal of Fe-CDs was detected in the supernatant by fluorometry and calculated as injected dosage per gram of the tissue sample (% ID/g).

Orthotopic tumor-bearing mice were arbitrarily divided into 5 groups (each group was n = 7). Intravenously (i.v.) administration of each group (N-CDs, Fe-CDs, Fe-CDs@Ang, and free Ang@Mal-PEG2k-NHS (Polymers conjugated with angiopeptide), or PBS every other day. A total of 10 mg CDs Equiv./kg per mice were injected through the i.v. tail vein 6 times on an alternate day from day 12 after tumor implantation. The mice were anesthetized using isoflurane after the injection (48 h), and their tumor luminescence intensity was observed via the Lumina IVIS III system. The relative photon flux was determined by normalized to the initial intensity and expressed as I/I0, where I0 is the bio-luminescence of the tumor on day 0. The body weight of mice was independently quantified and further normalized to their initial weight at 0 days. The survival assays were determined for 56 days (n = 6) by applying the Kaplan–Meier survival curve. On day 23, the therapy was stopped, and one mouse from each group was sacrificed for further immunohistochemistry and TUNEL assays.

Flow cytometric analyses were performed with Cytexpert and FlowJo X 10.0.7 software. All the statistical analyses (ANOVAs (one-way and two-way) with Tucky multiple comparisons tests and Student’s t-tests) were all performed using OriginPro 2019 (evaluation version). * p < 0.05, ** p < 0.01, *** p < 0.001 indicating the corresponding significant levels. All error bars show the mean± SEM as unless otherwise indicated. The survival results were analyzed using the Kaplan-Meier technique by OriginPro 2019 software. Biodistribution, relative photon flux, and bodyweight analysis were performed in Microsoft Excel 2016.
